Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: Step-change upward in monthly transaction volume from 2020 to 2021
Temporal Patterns.description: Monthly record counts rise sharply at the start of 2021 and remain at a much higher level throughout 2021 than in 2020.
Temporal Patterns.evidence: Counts are identical across variables. In 2020, monthly counts range from 60 (2020-06) to 150 (2020-01). In 2021, monthly counts range from 662 (2021-02) to 728 (2021-09 and 2021-10). The jump is visible from 92 in 2020-12 to 711 in 2021-01, and counts stay above 660 for every month in 2021.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: Any time-based comparison of sums between 2020 and 2021 is heavily influenced by much higher transaction volume in 2021.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: Total Sales sums are consistently much higher in 2021 than in 2020
Temporal Patterns.description: Monthly total sales show a clear year-over-year level shift upward in 2021 versus 2020.
Temporal Patterns.evidence: In 2020, monthly Total Sales sums range from 8026527 (2020-12) to 24607006 (2020-04). In 2021, monthly sums range from 39148532 (2021-03) to 78334681 (2021-07). Even the lowest 2021 month, 39148532 in 2021-03, exceeds the highest 2020 month, 24607006 in 2020-04.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: Revenue levels are materially higher in 2021, indicating a major change in business scale over time.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: Operating Profit sums are consistently much higher in 2021 than in 2020
Temporal Patterns.description: Monthly operating profit also shifts to a higher level in 2021 compared with 2020.
Temporal Patterns.evidence: In 2020, monthly Operating Profit sums range from 2292728 (2020-06) to 9301302 (2020-04). In 2021, monthly sums range from 14577799 (2021-03) to 29137256 (2021-07). The lowest 2021 month, 14577799 in 2021-03, is above the highest 2020 month, 9301302 in 2020-04.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: Profitability in absolute dollars improved substantially in 2021, not just sales.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: Units Sold sums are consistently much higher in 2021 than in 2020
Temporal Patterns.description: Monthly unit volume rises to a much higher level in 2021 and remains elevated all year.
Temporal Patterns.evidence: In 2020, monthly Units Sold sums range from 16269.0 (2020-06) to 56992.32 (2020-08). In 2021, monthly sums range from 142969.02 (2021-03) to 193266.15 (2021-08). The lowest 2021 month, 142969.02 in 2021-03, is well above the highest 2020 month, 56992.32 in 2020-08.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: The business sold far more units in 2021, supporting the higher sales and profit totals.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: Mid-2020 decline followed by partial rebound in Total Sales
Temporal Patterns.description: Total Sales fall sharply from spring into early summer 2020, rebound in late summer, then weaken again toward year-end.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: 2020 shows pronounced intra-year volatility, which matters for planning and benchmarking.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: Mid-2020 decline followed by partial rebound in Operating Profit
Temporal Patterns.description: Operating Profit follows a similar 2020 pattern: spring high, June trough, late-summer recovery, then weaker final quarter.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: Profit volatility mirrors revenue volatility in 2020, indicating unstable monthly performance.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: 2021 Total Sales trough in March followed by rise to July peak
Temporal Patterns.description: In 2021, Total Sales decline through March, then climb strongly through July before easing in August-October and rebounding in December.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: 2021 revenue shows a clear within-year cycle with strong summer and year-end performance.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: 2021 Operating Profit trough in March followed by rise to July peak
Temporal Patterns.description: Operating Profit in 2021 declines through March, rises strongly to July, softens in August-October, and rebounds by December.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: Profit follows a similar seasonal rhythm to sales in 2021, useful for forecasting earnings timing.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: Units Sold in 2021 rise into late summer, then dip in autumn
Temporal Patterns.description: Monthly unit volume in 2021 is lowest in March, increases through August, then declines in October-November before recovering in December.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: Demand volume appears strongest in late summer 2021 and weaker in early spring and autumn.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: Price per Unit mean is lower in early 2021 than in 2020, then rises through late 2021
Temporal Patterns.description: Average selling price per unit falls to its lowest levels in early 2021, then trends upward across the rest of 2021.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: Pricing was weakest in early 2021 and strengthened materially by year-end, affecting revenue per transaction.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: Total Sales mean declines from 2020 into early 2021, then recovers by mid-to-late 2021
Temporal Patterns.description: Average sales per record are relatively high in parts of 2020, fall to a low in early 2021, then recover later in 2021.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: Although total revenue rose in 2021, average revenue per record was compressed early in the year before improving.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: Operating Profit mean declines from 2020 into early 2021, then recovers later in 2021
Temporal Patterns.description: Average profit per record is higher in several 2020 months, reaches a low in early 2021, and then improves through mid and late 2021.
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: Per-transaction profitability was weakest in early 2021 and improved later, even while aggregate profit stayed high due to larger volume.

Temporal Patterns.pattern_name: Retailer ID mean remains nearly flat over time
Temporal Patterns.description: The monthly mean of Retailer ID changes only slightly across the full period, showing no strong temporal trend.
Temporal Patterns.evidence: Retailer ID mean stays in a narrow band from 1128299.0 (2020-06) to 1191781.5 (2020-09). In 2021 it remains tightly clustered between 1168978.27 (2021-01) and 1178933.37 (2021-09).
Temporal Patterns.business_relevance: This field appears temporally stable and is unlikely to be useful for identifying time-driven business performance changes.

Correlation Patterns.pattern_name: Total Sales and Operating Profit move together very strongly
Correlation Patterns.description: Total Sales and Operating Profit have a very strong positive relationship. Records with higher sales revenue also tend to have higher operating profit. Given the semantic definitions, this relationship is likely largely structural because profit is generated from sales after operating costs, and the relatively bounded Operating Margin distribution supports that profit tends to scale with sales.
Correlation Patterns.evidence: Pearson correlation r = 0.9563, a very strong positive correlation. As Total Sales increases, Operating Profit also increases strongly.
Correlation Patterns.business_relevance: This indicates that revenue growth is closely tied to profit growth in the dataset. From a business perspective, sales expansion appears to translate into operating profit consistently, making Total Sales a key indicator for profit performance.

Correlation Patterns.pattern_name: Units Sold is strongly associated with Total Sales
Correlation Patterns.description: Units Sold and Total Sales show a strong positive relationship. Transactions with more units sold tend to generate higher total sales. This relationship is likely partly structural because sales revenue is directly influenced by quantity sold, though variation in Price per Unit means it is not a perfect one-to-one relationship.
Correlation Patterns.evidence: Pearson correlation r = 0.8449, a strong positive correlation. Higher Units Sold is associated with higher Total Sales.
Correlation Patterns.business_relevance: This suggests sales volume is a major driver of revenue. Operationally, actions that increase unit volume are likely to have a meaningful impact on total sales outcomes.

Correlation Patterns.pattern_name: Units Sold is strongly associated with Operating Profit
Correlation Patterns.description: Units Sold and Operating Profit have a strong positive relationship. Records with higher quantities sold tend to produce higher operating profit. This dependency is likely partly structural and partly business-behavioral: more units sold raises revenue, which in turn supports higher profit, while margin variation prevents the relationship from being perfect.
Correlation Patterns.evidence: Pearson correlation r = 0.8139, a strong positive correlation. Higher Units Sold is associated with higher Operating Profit.
Correlation Patterns.business_relevance: This shows that volume growth is not only linked to revenue but also to profitability. Increasing sales quantity appears to be an important lever for improving operating profit in this dataset.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Retailer volume is concentrated in three retailers
Grouping Patterns.description: Units sold are heavily concentrated in West Gear, Foot Locker, and Sports Direct, while Walmart and Amazon are much smaller by total volume.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Total units sold by retailer: West Gear 619822.445, Foot Locker 569453.95, Sports Direct 553494.15, Kohl's 287375.0, Amazon 197990.0, Walmart 193412.825. West Gear sells about 3.21× Walmart (619822.445 vs 193412.825) and about 3.13× Amazon (619822.445 vs 197990.0). The top three retailers together total 1742770.545 units versus 678777.825 for the other three.
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: This concentration is meaningful because a small set of retailers accounts for most unit volume, so retailer partnerships, inventory allocation, and account management should prioritize these dominant channels.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Walmart has the highest average units sold per record despite the smallest record count
Grouping Patterns.description: Walmart stands out as a high-intensity retailer on a per-record basis, even though it has the fewest observations.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Mean units sold by retailer: Walmart 308.966, Kohl's 279.005, Sports Direct 272.389, West Gear 261.088, Foot Locker 215.948, Amazon 208.630. Walmart also has the lowest count at 626 records, versus West Gear 2374 and Foot Locker 2637. Walmart's mean is about 1.48× Amazon's (308.966 vs 208.630).
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: This is meaningful because Walmart appears to generate larger orders per record, which may support different replenishment, packaging, or channel strategies than higher-frequency but lower-intensity retailers.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Amazon has the highest average price per unit among retailers
Grouping Patterns.description: Average selling price differs across retailers, with Amazon at the top and Sports Direct at the bottom.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Mean price per unit by retailer: Amazon 48.760, Walmart 47.184, West Gear 46.738, Foot Locker 44.785, Kohl's 44.609, Sports Direct 42.047. Amazon's average price is 6.713 higher than Sports Direct's and about 1.16× as high (48.760 vs 42.047).
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: This matters for pricing and channel strategy because retailer channels are not priced evenly; Amazon supports the highest average unit price while Sports Direct is the lowest-priced among the listed retailers.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Regional unit volume is led by the West, but per-record intensity is led by the Southeast
Grouping Patterns.description: The West has the largest total units sold, while the Southeast has the highest average units sold per record.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Regional total units sold: West 684912.075, South 489063.2, Northeast 471618.69, Midwest 391337.0, Southeast 384617.405. Regional mean units sold: Southeast 314.230, South 283.023, West 279.784, Midwest 209.048, Northeast 198.493. The West leads total volume, but the Southeast leads per-record volume by 34.445 units over the West (314.230 vs 279.784).
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: This is meaningful because total demand and transaction intensity point to different regions. The West is the largest market by volume, while the Southeast appears to have larger orders per record.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Price levels are much higher in Southeast and West than in South and Midwest
Grouping Patterns.description: Regional pricing is uneven, with Southeast and West clearly above South and Midwest on average price per unit.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Mean price per unit by region: Southeast 50.183, West 49.941, Northeast 46.691, Midwest 40.362, South 38.237. Southeast is 11.946 higher than South and about 1.31× South's average price (50.183 vs 38.237). West is 9.579 higher than Midwest (49.941 vs 40.362).
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: This is meaningful for regional pricing and assortment decisions because the data shows materially different price levels across regions, not a uniform national pricing pattern.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Northeast has many records but the lowest average units sold among major regions
Grouping Patterns.description: The Northeast combines a high number of records with relatively low units sold per record.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Northeast has 2376 records, second only to West at 2448, but its mean units sold is 198.493, the lowest among all regions. By comparison, Southeast has only 1224 records but a much higher mean of 314.230. Northeast's mean is about 0.63× Southeast's (198.493 vs 314.230).
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: This matters because the Northeast appears to generate many smaller transactions, which can affect logistics, staffing, and sales planning differently from regions with fewer but larger transactions.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Product categories split into higher-priced apparel and lower-priced footwear
Grouping Patterns.description: Apparel categories have the highest average prices per unit, while footwear categories are lower-priced on average.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Mean price per unit by product: Women's Apparel 51.601, Men's Apparel 50.322, Men's Street Footwear 44.237, Men's Athletic Footwear 43.780, Women's Athletic Footwear 41.113, Women's Street Footwear 40.252. Women's Apparel is 11.348 higher than Women's Street Footwear and about 1.28× as high (51.601 vs 40.252).
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: This is meaningful because pricing differs systematically by product segment, supporting category-specific pricing, promotion, and margin management rather than a one-size-fits-all approach.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Men's Street Footwear is the dominant product by units sold
Grouping Patterns.description: Among product groups, Men's Street Footwear clearly leads both total units sold and average units sold per record.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Total units sold by product: Men's Street Footwear 555462.895, Men's Athletic Footwear 429482.05, Women's Apparel 423612.225, Women's Street Footwear 389072.2, Women's Athletic Footwear 317236.0, Men's Apparel 306683.0. Mean units sold by product: Men's Street Footwear 345.008, Men's Athletic Footwear 266.759, Women's Apparel 263.440, Women's Street Footwear 241.960, Women's Athletic Footwear 197.532, Men's Apparel 190.961. Men's Street Footwear total is about 1.81× Men's Apparel (555462.895 vs 306683.0).
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: This is meaningful because one product segment is the clear volume leader, which can guide assortment depth, forecasting, and promotional focus.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Women's Apparel pairs the highest average price with strong unit demand
Grouping Patterns.description: Women's Apparel has the highest average price per unit and still ranks near the top in units sold.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Women's Apparel has the highest mean price per unit at 51.601. Its total units sold are 423612.225, ranking third behind Men's Street Footwear 555462.895 and Men's Athletic Footwear 429482.05, and its mean units sold is 263.440. This places it above both Women's Street Footwear (389072.2 units; mean 241.960) and Women's Athletic Footwear (317236.0 units; mean 197.532) despite the higher price.
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: This is meaningful because Women's Apparel combines premium pricing with substantial volume, making it an important category for revenue and margin attention.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: State-level unit sales are highly uneven, with a few states far ahead
Grouping Patterns.description: A small set of states dominates total units sold, while several states are much smaller.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Top state totals for units sold include California 162260.025, Texas 156303.2, New York 139479.69, Florida 122700.74. Lower totals include Nebraska 19154.0, Maryland 20818.0, Minnesota 20838.0, Maine 22410.0. California sells about 8.47× Nebraska (162260.025 vs 19154.0).
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: This concentration is meaningful for territory planning and inventory deployment because the largest states materially outweigh the smallest in total unit demand.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Some states combine high prices with low volume, while others combine low prices with high volume
Grouping Patterns.description: State-level pricing and volume are not aligned uniformly; there are clear high-price/low-volume and low-price/high-volume examples.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Washington has the highest mean price per unit at 67.653 with mean units sold of 316.403. Utah has the lowest mean price at 26.829 with mean units sold of 224.759. Hawaii is also high-priced at 64.250 with mean units sold 280.382. South Carolina has the highest mean units sold at 452.274 with mean price 47.715, while North Carolina has mean units sold 422.660 with mean price 44.479. Pennsylvania and Rhode Island have very low mean units sold, 128.065 and 127.190 respectively, with mean prices near 49.940 and 40.352.
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: This is meaningful because states do not follow a single price-volume pattern, so state-level pricing and demand planning should be localized rather than assumed to move together.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: City-level counts are uneven, with Portland and Charleston standing out
Grouping Patterns.description: Most cities have 144 or 216 records, but Portland and Charleston have noticeably more observations.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: City record counts: Portland 360, Charleston 288, several large cities such as New York 216, Los Angeles 216, Houston 216, while many others have 144. Portland has 2.5× the records of a 144-count city and 1.67× the records of a 216-count city.
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: This matters because city-level comparisons should account for uneven representation; cities with more records may contribute more to totals simply due to more observations.

Grouping Patterns.pattern_name: Highest-volume cities are not the highest-priced cities
Grouping Patterns.description: Cities with the largest average units sold are different from those with the highest average prices per unit.
Grouping Patterns.evidence: Highest mean units sold cities include Miami 433.373, Charlotte 422.660, Houston 403.357, San Francisco 397.574, Albany 397.125. Highest mean price cities include Seattle 67.653, Honolulu 64.250, Orlando 60.616, Denver 60.090, Anchorage 57.215. Miami's mean price is 50.813, while Seattle's mean units sold is 316.403. Charlotte's mean price is 44.479, well below Seattle's 67.653, despite Charlotte's much higher mean units sold (422.660 vs 316.403).
Grouping Patterns.business_relevance: This is meaningful because pricing leaders and volume leaders are different city groups, suggesting separate strategies for premium pricing markets versus high-throughput markets.

Anomaly Patterns.description: Units Sold shows a noticeable upper-tail outlier pattern, with unusually large transaction quantities relative to the central distribution.
Anomaly Patterns.evidence: Outlier bounds are -225 to 655, and 627 records (6.50%) fall outside these bounds. Since the minimum is 0, the flagged outliers are effectively high-end values above 655. The distribution is right-skewed (skewness 1.356), with mean 250.99 above median 175, max 975, and IQR from 105 to 325.
Anomaly Patterns.business_relevance: Likely a real business-volume effect rather than a data quality issue, because values are nonnegative and the anomaly is concentrated in the upper tail. Downstream analysis should use robust summaries or winsorization if modeling average transaction size, while preserving these records for demand and large-order analysis.

Anomaly Patterns.pattern_name: Extreme right tail in Total Sales
Anomaly Patterns.description: Total Sales has a strongly uneven distribution with very large high-end values compared with the typical record, indicating revenue spikes or a mixture of small and very large transactions.
Anomaly Patterns.evidence: 653 records (6.77%) are outside the IQR-based bounds of -214363.75 to 368618.25; because the minimum is 0, these are upper-tail outliers above 368618.25. Mean is 93273.44 while median is only 9576.0, q25 is 4254.5, q75 is 150000.0, max is 825000.0, skewness is 1.841, and kurtosis is 3.144.
Anomaly Patterns.business_relevance: This is more consistent with real sales spikes or heterogeneous transaction sizes than with impossible values, since sales are nonnegative and the tail is on the high side. Use log transforms, robust statistics, or segment-level analysis in downstream work; do not remove by default because these records may represent major revenue events.

Anomaly Patterns.pattern_name: Highly concentrated extreme values in Operating Profit
Anomaly Patterns.description: Operating Profit contains a pronounced concentration of unusually high values, with a heavier tail than Total Sales and a very large gap between typical and average profit.
Anomaly Patterns.evidence: 706 records (7.32%) are outside the IQR bounds of -73289.5 to 127274.5; with minimum 0, the outliers are upper-tail values above 127274.5. Mean is 34425.28 versus median 4371.5, q25 is 1922.0, q75 is 52063.0, max is 390000.0, skewness is 2.234, and kurtosis is 5.820, the highest kurtosis among the reported business measures.
Anomaly Patterns.business_relevance: This pattern likely reflects real profit spikes tied to large or high-margin transactions rather than obvious data errors, because values are nonnegative and the tail is one-sided. For downstream analysis, use robust estimators and consider separate treatment of high-profit records in forecasting or profitability segmentation.

Anomaly Patterns.pattern_name: Price per Unit has limited but distinct upper-end price anomalies
Anomaly Patterns.description: Price per Unit is relatively stable overall but includes a small set of unusually high prices above the normal range.
Anomaly Patterns.evidence: Only 81 records (0.84%) are outside the IQR bounds of 5.0 to 85.0. The observed minimum is 7.0, so outliers are only on the high side above 85.0. Mean is 45.22, median 45.0, q25 35.0, q75 55.0, and max 110.0, with mild right skewness of 0.362.
Anomaly Patterns.business_relevance: Because the outlier share is small and the overall distribution is fairly centered, these values are more likely premium pricing or product/channel differences than data quality issues. Keep them in analysis, but check whether pricing models should cap or segment high-price records.

Anomaly Patterns.pattern_name: Operating Margin has a small set of boundary-like extreme values
Anomaly Patterns.description: Operating Margin is mostly compact but includes a small number of unusually low and high margin records near the edges of the observed range.
Anomaly Patterns.evidence: 44 records (0.46%) are outside the IQR bounds of 14.0 to 70.0. Observed values range from 10.0 to 80.0, so the anomalies are values below 14 and above 70. Mean is 42.30, median 41.0, q25 35.0, q75 49.0, with low skewness 0.227 and low kurtosis 0.175.
Anomaly Patterns.business_relevance: These could be real unusually low- or high-margin transactions, but because margin is a ratio-like measure, edge values should be validated for calculation consistency. Downstream analysis should retain them but consider sensitivity checks, especially in margin benchmarking.

Anomaly Patterns.pattern_name: Zero-inflation at the lower bound in sales and profit measures
Anomaly Patterns.description: Several business measures have a minimum of exactly zero despite substantial positive upper tails, indicating a pile-up at the lower boundary.
Anomaly Patterns.evidence: Units Sold min = 0, Total Sales min = 0, and Operating Profit min = 0. At the same time, these variables have large positive maxima (975, 825000, and 390000 respectively) and strong right skewness (1.356, 1.841, and 2.234).
Anomaly Patterns.business_relevance: The exact count of zeros is not provided, so zero-inflation cannot be quantified precisely, but the boundary mass is plausible for no-sale or zero-profit records. This is more likely a real business state than a data error. Downstream analysis should explicitly account for zeros, for example with two-part models or separate treatment of zero vs positive records.

Anomaly Patterns.pattern_name: Retailer ID is heavily concentrated on a single value
Anomaly Patterns.description: Retailer ID shows an unexpectedly concentrated distribution for an identifier field, with the middle 50% collapsing to one value.
Anomaly Patterns.evidence: Median, q25, and q75 are all 1185732.0, meaning at least 50% of records share that exact ID. The range is 1128299.0 to 1197831.0, mean is 1173849.72, and skewness is -1.081.
Anomaly Patterns.business_relevance: For an identifier, this concentration is unusual and may indicate dataset dominance by one retailer, repeated records from one entity, or possible encoding/coverage imbalance rather than a numeric outlier issue. It should not be treated as a continuous variable in modeling; instead, validate category balance and consider stratified analysis by retailer.
